title: Extreme Caution - Very Low Protein is only maybe for very late stage CKD-
body: This writer is NOT a doctor, and especially NOT a Kidney Doctor. He is selling a product. I'm glad I kept seeking answers because this Very Low Protein diet is (maybe) only if you are about to go on dialysis. At the early stages this diet can harm our health unnecessarily. 'Learn the facts about kidney disease", sold here on Amazon is highly recommended, written by Dr. Steven Ro. . . md. Also, dadvice on youtube hosts this doctor once a month live and he gives a talk and then does live Q and A. What I have learned is that ckd is very complex info, but regular doctors don't really know it deeply, and you don't get a referral until late stage. GFR is a number from a blood test, a guide. But the most important thing to know is if you have protein in your urine - because that means the kidney is damaged no matter what the gfr. Also, if you are older then it's normal to have a lower gfr. The risks of ckd is it often causes artery disease and heart attacks and strokes. So often very safe blood pressure meds help prevent damage. As to diet, it seems that a plant based Pattern of diet - like the Mediterranean etc. is very helpful. So, lots of fruits veggies, beans, whole grain, low saturated fat animal protein, fatty fish, good oils and nuts etc. Dietary protein is not the same as protein in urine. This other doctor says that if you have diabetes - a major cause of kidney disease and you eat the Very Low Protein with the amino (this book), it will be very bad for your health. He recommends 60 to 80 grams a day protein, as Americans eat too much. Also, if you have protein in urine, low salt helps. Restricting potassium, phosphorous etc as written in this book, is ONLY for the latter stages Stage 4 and Stage 5. At these stages you will get a referral to a specialized kidney doctor, and a dietician. I rarely give reviews, but I wanted to help other people navigate thru learning about kidney disease. Best of luck, try the other book first, and utube of ACTUAL kidney doctors (I've found 2 0r 3 that are very good) - NOT people selling supplements or who aren't medical experts on Kidneys specifically.

body: I like to analyze and be informed. Recently diagnosed with Stage 3 Kidney Disease in November. Not efficiently summarized for lay person and I needed to breakdown information and summarize and presented to friends with only about 50 pages. Using this information and Keto Diet information I have reversed Kidney Disease to Stage 2 and lost 25 pounds in 3 months. It’s all about diet. Didn’t know I had so much waste or uremic acid in bloodstream from excessive eating! It was too much for kidneys to expel. Next time you see someone with fat belly or swollen eyelids or swollen areas below eyes, think about what it could mean. Uremic acid can circulate in brain and cause dementia. It’s all about what and how much you eat! I’m guilty!

author: A. Purse
title: This book is ACES!
body: My doctors have been reading my kidney tests for years and saying everything is fine. I switched from Blue Shield to Kaiser and what Kaiser does is send you your lab results directly and to the doctor. I learned from Kaiser that my kidney function is at 58%. I am 63 and I find that very concerning. I was taking lithium I’m a bipolar person and it adversely affected my kidneys. I stopped taking lithium and modified with the help of my psychiatrist and I bought this book. I started watching my phosphorus my protein and my sodium and I just got off the phone with my nephrologist and my kidney function on my last test was 78%! That is a dramatic improvement and I owe it 100% to this book. Not one doctor suggested that I should modify not one doctor told me I was at 58%. You have to find and fix your own problems you cannot rely on doctors to do it for you. The doctors don’t care if I die at 75 from kidney disease. I do. I don’t want to die early because I’m bipolar and have to take medication. I changed my meds with kidney function in mind and have modified my diet. This was a huge improvement and gives me great comfort. I’m so grateful for this book I can’t even tell you. Lee Hull has done your research for you he has backed up every word he said with quantifiable scientific research and that’s what I respect. Just the facts I’ll make my own decisions but give me the facts. I wish you the best in your kidney journey and this book will definitely definitely help.

body: This is the best and most comprehensive book on CKD that I've ever read. Yes it is technical; however, it fully explained what was happening to me both physically and psychologically, and it gave me confidence that I could take control and slow the progression of this disease. Not only have my labs improved, it is doubtful that I will ever need dialysis from following Lee's recommendations. Lee is no b.s. and walks the talk. He seems sincere about helping other kidney patients. I also take his supplements.

author: Peter Rogers MD
title: Great book!
body: Hull is a kidney patient who is trying to save himself. He energetically read through the literature on maintaining renal function, restored his own labs to normal, and wrote this book. Hull did a heroic job with this book! Hull leaves no stone unturned in trying to optimize renal function. All renal insufficiency patients should read it. Weaknesses of the book are that it is too long winded. It could have been 50% shorter. I am a physician expert in vascular disease and nutrition. I recommend that you watch the videos at my you tube channel "Peter Rogers MD," then read this book. Hull goes into far more detail, than I do, but I'm better at getting to the key points fast. Understanding nutrition is key for protecting the kidneys. What do the kidneys do? The kidneys excrete nitrogen, and buffer pH. Where does nitrogen come from? Nitrogen comes from protein. Where does acid come from? Acid comes primarily from the amino acids in protein. How can you make life easier for your kidneys? Eat less protein! Especially less animal protein, because it is more acidic. These concepts and other like them can potentially help maintain your kidneys for multiple extra years before dialysis. Best wishes

author: B. Terry
title: This Book Could Save Your Life!
body: I was told by my doctor that my Kidneys were not functioning as good as they should be for someone my age and fitness level. So she told me to eat more veggies and lower my protein. So I stopped eating meat, only having a little every 3 weeks..No red meat at all. I increased my veggies and fruit. I was not too worried about it so I was not really strict about it as I should have been… Until I went to get my Covid booster and the nurse from another Primary Health location told me you have Chronic Kidney Disease STAGE2..I was Blown away!!! I was I not told this?? So I started to do my own reachers and really started to watch my diet. I had two weeks till I went in for my blood work to see if my GFR had improved. I also have high cholesterol. I was so relieved that my GFR was up, but still not normal and my cholesterol was down, but still not normal. That is when I decided to see what I could find about this disease and see if I could do anything to help myself. Stopping Kidney Disease book has been a EYE OPENER! I know now why I have high cholesterol, and bone loss. Kidney disease can and will cause all kinds of health problems if not taken seriously, by educating yourself and making changes now!! Don't wait till you are a Stage 3 or 4. Doctors don't seem to know anything about how serious this disease is. They will not care until you are in Stage 4 and 5..which is horrible to think that this where I was headed if I had not found this book. I know Stopping Kidney Disease has given me the knowledge to make the right changes. Do not just google and follow the foods that are recommended, a lot of them are wrong.

title: A relief to have an in depth explanation of kidney disease.
body: I am very glad I found this after blood work showed I have stage 3 CKD. So far it has explained so much of what I have been experiencing in the past couple of years as far as changes in my body (loss of muscle, which I was trying to treat by eating more protein!), my urine (foamy), my energy levels (less), and more, all of which my MD could offer no good explanation. It doesn't focus on the anatomy & physiology of the process, but on what the relevant effects of diet and exercise are on your kidneys. You are what you eat! Still, many chapters are frustrating to read as they are poorly edited; sometimes do not offer an explanation of abbreviations, and you will find contradictions in a paragraph due to these errors. I have read about half the book so far. There is a lot to take in, some days I read just one chapter. Plan to get the diet and recipe book because it seems there isn't a whole lot of foods that are helpful to the kidneys.

author: Paul Simister
title: Hard to read and understand but very important
body: This is a long, technical book about doing what you can to slow or stop kidney disease from progressing, and even perhaps to put it into reverse by eating the right food and avoiding ingredients that harm your kidneys. It is therefore based on willpower and how well you can stick to the guidance. For me to do this, it's essential to understand why. Why I need to take action in stage 3 (to slow down progress to stage 4 and beyond) and why the diet says "don't eat X, Y and X" and "do eat A, B and C". I appreciate all the explanations and links to the research. The book was published in 2019, so it's quite up to date (I'm writing in early 2021). However, the book is a complicated read because of the multiple stages of kidney disease so only some of the research will be very relevant to you now. Much of it is hard to read and at a level I don't understand. The big message I'm getting is that meat and other animal products are bad for people with CKD. High in protein, acidic, high in nitrogen... I have criticisms. The book is very good for explaining why you shouldn't eat X because of risk factor/condition A etc. It's not good at pulling everything together. I've finished up very confused. I want a list of core ingredients I can use safely and others I can eat in moderation, either through small quantities or by making sure that if ingredient B is an issue in problem C, I must combine it with other ingredients that are low on that area. The author has developed a nitrogen free protein pill to top up protein requirements. On the one hand, it is good that someone is working on solutions, on the other, you can feel cynical about some of the content. Having checked what the NHS and UK kidney charities are saying about diet, this book is very extreme. I think it's great that it makes you think about what you eat. Unfortunately I'm left feeling frightened about eating pretty much everything because I've lost sight of the big picture. I am reducing the amount of animal protein I eat and I'll see what happens to my blood results.

author: Smarter Now
title: What your doctor won't tell you
body: I am 82 years old. I was diagnosed with stage 3 kidney disease in September of 2020 after being told for months before that that my falling eGFR was just due to congestive heart failure. After a week in the hospital in mid-October for what apparently was a multiple failure of body parts to work right, I, after 8 years of being vegan, decided that I needed more animal protein and went back to eating animals, mainly fish and chicken. During this period of time I noticed that my urine had a peculiar strong smell that lingered in the bathroom, but then I noticed that on days when I had little protein, the smell was gone. This led me to Hull's book. I am back on a low protein vegan diet and my eGFR has gone from a low of 30 in the hospital to 57. In recent blood tests, the eGFR went from 44 to 57 in two weeks after the diet change. When I told the nephrologist what I was doing, she was all for it and very supportive, but I suspect that if she stepped out of the "approved" narrative for the treatment of kidney disease in the medical community, she'd be facing rough criticism. In my opinion, the medical world in it's extreme reluctance to embrace change is often doing a real disservice to those who rely on it for competent advice and guidance.. Eating a low protein diet is not easy, especially if one has been used to meat-based meals. but I see this as an alternative to dialysis, clearly a life-style altering treatment. Keeping one's diet to under 30 grams of protein daily is a challenge. You will be surprised at how much protein there is in wheat and many vegetables and other grains. I you're looking for more good years of life with kidney disease, I heartily recommend this book and his other little book, Stopping Kidney Disease Food Guide, that has recipes that can give you an idea of how to pursue this kind of diet.

author: BJL
title: BEST KIDNEY BOOK ON THE MARKET - A MUST READ IF YOU HAVE CKD OR WANT TO PREVENT IT
body: Being a very scientific and analytical person, I enjoy reading medical journals and professional articles. I tend to question everything a medical professional prescribes to me and my family. This book is a dream come true for me since there are hundreds of references to medical articles, charts and graphs. By far, author Lee Hull saved me thousands of hours of research. In this book you'll learn: Factors and foods that increase the speed of kidney disease progression Factors and foods that slow down the progression of kidney disease Please note that although the book utilizes scientific data, it is written in layman's language. Very easy to read and understand. It is now a permanent part of my home library and I plan to refer to it over and over. I highly recommend this book even if you own other kidney disease books and cookbooks. Thank you, Lee Hull, for writing this book that will benefit many kidney patients.

author: D. M. Kimura
title: The must-read book for anyone with all stages of kidney disease
body: Lee Hull did all of the heavy lifting in sourcing the research and becoming a human guinea pig in what worked for him. For anyone like me who received an elevated creatinine test result, the book is best resource for understanding the cause and effect of what helps and damages your kidneys. My creatinine levels have dropped and I am asymptomatic after 3 months. He provides guidance, resources, but mostly a shift giving the patient control over how they want to maintain or improve their kidney function.

author: Susan
title: Literally A Lifesaver
body: I want to add my praise to Lee Hull for writing this book. When I was diagnosed with Stage 3 kidney disease, my nephrologist told me just to eat a healthy diet and then we would monitor my progression periodically. That was no advice at all. His office offered no referrals to dietitians or to kidney diets. When I asked for my latest blood work, I was even quizzed as to why. Since then, I have had to research on my own and learn to read my own blood work. Lee Hull’s book offers scientific advice on his similar struggle. I highly recommend it. It has saved my sanity and will possibly save my life!

author: University Doc
title: interesting and informative! Highly recommend for other CKD patients....
body: This is an interesting and informative book! Great book for those with Chronic Kidney Disease and those who care/cook for them. Highly recommend for other CKD patients. Dialysis and transplant are not the only options available even though Nephologists want you to think so. This book delves into the third alternative, a change in diet and nutrition can help delay or stop progression of kidney disease. The author and fellow patient with CKD does and excellent job of research and presenting the information. His writing is backed up by thorough research and medical studies. Low protein diets have been proven to halt or delay the need for dialysis and/or transplant. Dr. Mackenzie Walzer of John Hopkins University and one of his patients Tim Alstrom showed this. Here, Hull, another patient of Dr. Walzer's shows the same success with low protein diets. If you have chronic kidney disease, you have to read and practice what is in this book. I have already done began my treatment plan using this book as a guideline.

author: skunktrain
title: Such a Godsend!
body: I had a severe kidney infection when I was a kid (blood in urine, etc) but recovered. Ever since then, I've felt my kidneys were "delicate" but no doctor ever mentioned anything. I'm always told my kidney values are normal and nothing more. Last week I got a message from my doctor that my kidney labs were "a little low" and that I needed to do more tests, including an ultrasound. AND, that it was urgent that I get tested immediately! No other information was given and of course, I couldn't actually talk to the doctor to get more details. I was in a panic! I didn't know if my numbers were just a tiny bit off or if I was headed for dialysis. Because too many doctors don't tell you ANYTHING. I cannot say how much comfort this book has given me over this long week of stress and worry. I know that even *if* I have kidney disease, there is something I can DO ABOUT IT. I already have started to follow the recommended diet and my blood pressure readings are now lower than they have been in a long time and some mild edema in my ankles (that I've had consistently for over 15 years) has faded. I finally heard from my doctor's office: Not all the results of the new tests have come back (but the ultrasound is normal). However, the original tests that triggered all of this say that my BUN is normal, creatinine is slightly elevated, and GFR is 52. (Which would be Stage 3a kidney disease, meaning mild to moderate.) However, GFR readings are just a "snapshot" of the status of your kidneys, so I am not officially in Stage 3 until more time has passed. I have confidence that if I continue to follow the advice in this book that my GFR will improve and hopefully I can avoid being diagnosed with kidney disease completely. But even if I do have this disease, I feel more hope and confidence now. I believe that by following this book, I can protect my kidneys and prevent a hasty decline. If I don't have kidney disease, then I know what I need to do to take the strain and stress off of my delicate kidneys and maybe I'll never get this disease. Thank you so much, Lee Hull, for this book. God bless you. It has given so many of us hope and education.

author: RSP
title: LIFESAVER!!!
body: The size and font are great! My Mom is like a student in school again! She had NOTHING up in her area. She was so depressed and helpless. This book has saved her life!!! It is phenomenal!! Please please please get this for the people who are struggling in your life with diabetes and kidney failure. And thanks to everyone who took the time to do a review!!!

author: nASHVILLE NURSE
title: Very informative and written by someone who “understands “
body: Excellent. Very informative. As a nurse I appreciate all of his references to various studies. As a patient I appreciate his personal reflections and encouragement

author: john
title: Great book and information if you act on the advice.
body: This is the second book I have read on kidney disease and how to treat it and this is probably the best one I have found although the other book was good too. I was in stage 2 and had a GFR of 70 and a creatinine level of 1.30 and was falling fast when i first read this book. After about 3 weeks of following most of the advice in the book I had my blood work done and my GFR was at 104 and my creatinine had fallen to a .93. Needless to say I was pretty shocked because my GFR had been falling pretty quick on the previous 3 tests. I still have protein in my urine but I believe I may be greatly slowing my disease progression down by following the advice in this book.

author: C. Hobbins
title: DISSAPOINTED !
body: Sorry but this book is over one hundred pages of ok facts and figures that take hours and hours to plough through.... thoroughly researched ? Yes !.... but the reader anxious to find a dietary list a diet or a recipe will be bitterly disappointed.... unless of course they are a numbers geeks......I’m not..... It is a book that has excellent potential at first sight, but it could be cut down by over 80 per cent ..... I can recommend it fully as an aid to exercise.... carry it over your head... or....swing it around in a bag for a while. To summarise..... an extremely well researched book by a dedicated and knowledgeable author.... but, for the, ordinary guy or lass, it is a heavy tome filled with graphs, lists, more lists and check lists..... I wish I had spent my money on recommended vegetables and fruits . CH

author: Bev
title: Confusing and Too Technical
body: It reads like a medical book. Lots of studies that just make the book not very user friendly. I sent it back.

author: Patricia
title: Not user friendly
body: This would be a helpful and great resource if you have a medical degree. The end of chapter summaries are helpful but it’s not a book I would recommend

author: Carol A Hunt
title: If you have Kidney Disease this book is a must
body: All I can say is WOW tons of information and back up documentation. Lee did a great job with this book the best one I've read to date.

author: Rueben Woods
title: So details
body: retains a lots of knowledge

author: Di
title: Understanding the functioning of the kidneys & preserving them.
body: The book is knowledge on preserving the kidneys along with the cook book.

author: Wayne Lusvardi
title: Who can argue with this book if the writer went into remission with kidney disease?
body: Kidney patient and former investment advisor Lee Hull has written an ambitious book on his experience and research in stopping kidney disease. But the book is too long and technical and needed a good edit before publication. And despite my favorability toward the thrust of the book, it also deserves a constructive critique. First, Hull’s book is a challenge to Big Pharma and the technological paradigm for modern medical care. Hull, like myself and so many others, have gotten the run-around by the medical system when they discovered they had kidney disfunction. I bounced around over 30 years with over 25+ different kind of doctors with a syndrome of symptoms that the medical system never diagnosed. I finally had to abandon conventional medical care and seek assistance from naturopaths and functional medicine doctors. I had two heart attacks that occurred on hot days when I was dehydrated from exercise. When I had stents placed in arteries I told doctors I did not have high cholesterol as much as dehydration resulting in collapsed arteries. Ten years later I recognized that what I had was kidney-heart attacks. I was referred to a nephrologist who was entirely unhelpful and was seemingly waiting around for a high-tech and high cost fix of dialysis. Unfortunately, blood testing for kidney function is only for when the kidney is failing not for prevention or early detection. Hull does a lot toward plugging the gigantic hole of conventional medicine when it comes to prevention of kidney disfunction. Hull explains how the kidneys work and contributing factors to disease (e.g., low albumin, high renal acid, low protein, glycation, low phosphorous, killer calcium, low sodium, caloric and methionine reduction, anemia, high potassium, vitamin D and low magnesium). What this book shows is that a modern technological form of folk medicine offers more to people suffering with undiagnosed or diagnosed kidney disease than the conventional medical model. The strongest part of Hull’s book is that he recognizes that heart disease and kidney disease are often nearly one and the same. What Hull suggests is intriguing in that proteinuria (high protein in the urine) might be a better early marker for heart attack (myocardial infarctions) than troponin, an after-heart attack marker, (see page 91). All of the above said, the book deserves a constructive critique. Where I had trouble with Hull’s book, and almost all other health or medical advice books for that matter, is that patients typically present a syndrome or group of associated symptoms that occur together that a patient may view as separate (if genetic, known as Fabry Disease which is an enzyme deficiency not mentioned in Hull’s book). Back aches, fatigue, brain fog, dehydration, what seems like intolerance to all food, joint pain, gout, oxalates stones are typical. But telling doctors these symptoms early in the course of this disease will lead nowhere. Doctors only have markers for acute disease, not what appears as chronic disease. There is no billing code for the above-described syndrome. What this means is that using food or supplements for one symptom may not work for another. For example, Hull calls calcium a kidney killer as it leads to vascular calcification in his view. But apparently Hull has never had oxalate crystals or stones build up in his kidney and joints as his book does not even mention oxalate stones. What is counterintuitive is that calcium citrate needs to be ingested 20 minutes before eating to bind with oxalates in foods to remove them. Moreover, oxalate crystals can deposit in the rectum or intestinal tract leading to bleeding or leaky gut. So, avoiding calcium could promote stone formation. And taking one supplement, such as high doses of Vitamin C may actually increase oxalates. But Hull recommends higher doses of Vitamin C as an anti-oxidant (p. 144, 146). And he recommends to eat foods with low burnt charcoal or charred foods (called Advanced Glycation End Products or AGE). But, for example, cranberries might be low in AGE but are extremely high in oxalates. Hull is a former investment advisor. His 527-page book is organized like a stock or bond prospectus. What he might have done is organize foods and supplements into columns and cross classify them for glycation, oxalates, calcium, etc. Moreover, the book is way too long and intimidating for the average reader facing no real help from conventional medicine. Next time he writes a book he should hire a good editor. But who can argue with him if the contents of his book resulted in him going into remission as he states?

author: Dan & Marie
title: I have learned sooooooo much
body: This food as medicine journey should have begun 20 years ago! I love to cook and am now part of the slow food movement. My life will increase by years. It is essential reading for any one with CKD

author: Amazon Customer
title: Great source of information
body: I believe in a plant based diet and follow it at around 85%. It’s good to know I’m on the right track. I’ve been stage 3 and now I’m stage 2. I’m planning to reread this and apply more principles with the goal of being stage 1.

author: Wendom D. Palmer
body: Very difficult to understand

author: Joan
title: Very thorough--I recommend this book to others who are looking outside the box to avoid dialysis.
body: The author gives a very intelligent, comprehensive, research-backed and methodical overview of pertinent renal failure research and studies. At first glance it is intimidating because I am neither a researcher nor a scientist! However, he continues to give cogent advice that is clearly based on knowledge and is, himself, a testimony to the hope he offers to all those who are facing renal failure and possible dialysis. He has done what the doctors say is unrealistic: avoided dialysis! As the mother of a miracle son who had to be on dialysis as a baby and has had two kidney transplants, I am now on the warpath to save my husband's renal function, I am grateful to the author and feel hopeful that, in going outside the box, we will beat the odds! Thank you so very much for this guidebook and for the knowledge that it CAN be done!

author: Kindle Customer
title: Very scientific. Smart!
body: Lee brings hope to this kidney patient. I've tried a few changes with good results. It's not easy but dialysis will be much harder. Bravo to Lee for helping to bring all this data together in one book.

author: Coro
title: Great Book
body: It's 500 pages but well worth reading. Probably the best I've read. I highly recommend this book.

author: Dawn Zachary
title: Not what I expected, disappointed
body: Not easy to read. Wanted a guide or advise on "Stopping Kidney Disease. Not a bunch of mumbo jumbo.

author: Kindle Customer
title: Don’t waste your money!
body: Not a good read!

author: T W F
title: MUST HAVE!!!!
body: This is a MUST HAVE book in your arsenal to help with CKD. It's a lot to get through but you can skim over much. If you have CKD this this book TODAY and the companion cook book. My numbers have improved just in the short time that I have owned this book and the cookbook.

author: Katherine J. Painter
title: Huge Book! 520 PAGES but absolutely worth money.
body: I had been looking for documentation on how to stop kidney disease. I have been heading towards dyalisis but do not want to do this. My labs have actually been improving since Ive paid attention to phosphorouse and potassium. I wanted to know what else I can do to improve my kidney function. I am very happy with the book and its contents are extremely important to me. I have improved kidney function and who knows... i may be able to use info here in book. Very helpful to me, giving me more info that I thought I would find,. DOCUMENTATION!! I have been looking for helpful into vs. just "trying.. blindly proceeding in hoping vs. actually working with knowledge thank you!!

author: San
title: Great nfo.
body: Great nfo.

author: Amazon Customer
title: This book is for sure the real deal for knowing how to deal with kidney disease
body: Some real a** s*** in this book. The author speaks his mind for sure about true health and well being. This defiantly needs to be read by everyone with a kidney condition. His researching and experimenting on himself provides him with such good usable knowledge I wish I had found this resource sooner. Way more useful then anything I have ever been told by a nephrologist or nutritionist. Having a kidney disease for +10 years now I know from experience that his advice is spot on.

author: Helen M. Binder
title: Very comprehensive book!
body: The book said how certain fruits can be deadly to kidney patients! Very informative info throughout book!

author: DrJ
title: Very detailed book
body: Suggestions seem to work & are consistent with other writers, eg esseltyne & Caldwell. BUN & creatinine saw improvement in weeks on veggie/fruit diet. But what to do with the frail muscle wasting patient that needs protein? The answer appears to be amino acids & plant protein. But I am Still implementing & evaluating Hull’s strategy, as he appears to be committed to this research & is the real McCoy when it comes to kidney disease from his own battle with this condition. I am planning on reading esseltyne & Greger & Caldwell, again; forks Over Knives, China Study & how to reverse heart disease.

author: Patty A. Haag
title: Helps understand kidney disease better
body: Good book

author: Aiden Jael Temple Paige
title: If you are encountering kidney disease buy this book.
body: I was recently and unexpectedly diagnosed with stage 3 CKD. This book has done much to explain my disease, how to treat it and it has given me a route forward and relieved my anxiety.

author: Karen L. Dunleavy
title: A Good Book but..
body: The main thing about the book I did not like was how heavy it was. It gave me neck issues trying to read it. I could not carry it with me anywhere. It has some good advice and I skipped around in it but like others (even though I can usually pick up medical and health advice easily for the most part) It was so overwhelming and too much with the technical aspect to get much out of it. I do know this is a very good book and is thorough on all the aspects of this disease, but it was taking so much time to even read parts of it and if I read the whole thing I would be dead by the time I did finish it or figured out what was being said.

author: Jerry Ahearn
title: Challenging to read, but a real eye-opener!
body: Hi everyone! -- I have been a type 2 diabetic for 25 years now, following the ADA recommendations regarding post-meal target goals and diet, which focused primarily on low-to-moderate GI food values, and allowed lean white meats, fish, supposedly healthy grains, some dairy and modest fruit intake, with an A1c target of 7.0% -- In retrospect, BIG mistake! Two months ago, my GP down here in Costa Rica -- I'm 73 and have been retired down here for just about 8 years now -- tells me off-handedly in Spanish -- NO details! -- that I have kidney disease and need to see a specialist in mid-November, 2019! I was STUNNED! The next day, after panicking a bit, I saw Lee's book and have read it 3 times since then, even though parts of it are full of somewhat torturous, medical gobbledegook. Thank goodness for my Chemistry Minor, Calculus and Statistics classes 50 years ago! Based on Lee's action plan at the end of the book, I decided to try a strict Vegan diet, with emphasis on the PRAL, alkaline food values along with plant protein to give my kidneys a reduced urea nitrogen load, increase my albumin, and eGFR -- currently 43 = stage 3b kidney disease -- while lowering my relatively high BUN level -- 24 -- and creatinine -- 1.65 -- as well. So far, I can only say VERY positively that I have lost 13 lbs/6 kilos -- BMI now 21, down from 23 -- I'm one of those statistically atypical "thin" type 2 diabetics -- no longer suffer painful diabetic neuropathy in my feet or hands, have increased my chronic inability to sleep at any given stretch from 3 1/2 hours max to 6 hours -- this alone has been well worth the effort! -- and I am projecting an A1c in the high 5's or lower 6's, down from 7.2% last July. Since I have been very strict with the diet -- mostly raw food, no meat, no fish, no dairy, no grains, rice, bread, cookies, sweets, apple strudel, fried food, diet colas or liquor, etc -- I have never smoked -- I feel cautiously optomistic that the renal numbers will improve as well in October! I elected to try things at first WITHOUT Albutrux, partially to see how effective the dietary intervention would be by itself, partially due to the cost of Albutrix, plus the lack of independent, critical scientific reviews on the Internet about the product, where and by whom it is produced. In my opinion Lee has done an anazing amount of valuable research -- God bless him for it! -- but I feel he could be a but more transparent about the provenance of his product, especially after his well-taken, cautionary chapter on the foibles and downright fraud in the nutitional supplement industry! I reason that I can always try it as a next step refinement, if needed, in the next cycle or so. Other people seem to be reporting what look like very good anecdotal results on Lee's website.The only speed bump? Dizziness and falling 3 times because my blood pressure went from borderline HIGH to clinically LOW due to Glibencamide and a B 12 vitamin deficiency, probably caused by the diet and Metformin. I quickly resolved it by immediately stopping the Glibencamide -- known to cause dizziness, although my young GP didn't seem to know that! -- and taking a B 12 oral supplement once a day. I will give a renal update around October 25th! -- ¡Pura Vida! -- Jerry Ahearn. ---------------------------------------------- UPDATE: Sept. 25, 2919 -- Hi everyone! -- Today is exactly 2 months since I bought Lee's book and formulated a "full court press" Vegan dietary action plan following his recommendations. I would say that I was 95% compliant with my goals during this period. NOT easy at times, but definitely doable. My curiosity finally got the best of me this morning-- I couldn't wait another month for my subsidized, goverment-administrated lab tests -- so I went to a private lab for a creatinine test and my eGFR has now IMPROVED from 42% to 52%, so I am now only stage 3a and hope to KEEP improving during the next cycle! What a relief! -- And, according to the eGFR calculator on the Internet provided by the National Kidney Foundation, my kidney function would have risen to 54% if I had not lost 13 lbs, but I'm not going to complain. The program seems to WORK!! -- Since I intend to have this test done privately each month out-of-pocket, I'll try to give another update next month!☺

author: Donna Caissie
title: CAUTION!
body: This review has been withdrawn due to the threat of legal action. I used to be a fan, and I bought the book, but when I questioned Lee about proof that his supplement works, he cited the generalized studies in his book -- none of which were done with his supplement. When I asked where his supplement was manufactured, he said in an FDA approved facility. When I asked for the name of the facility so I could check it out on the FDA website, my question was ignored. Lee's lack of transparency leaves a bad taste in my mouth, and that is why his book sits on my shelf gathering dust.

author: Linda L. Knudsen
title: Great resource for stopping kidney disease.
body: This book helped me face the reality that no doctor really expects you to conquer CKD. It convinced me that diet is most important to my health and that protein needs to be monitored. I appreciate the fact that it was written by a patient and widely researched. Was beginning my own research when I came across this book. A lot of material to get through but the chapter summaries are greatly appreciated and very helpful. My mind tends to wander when I read. Thank you Mr. Hull. Just purchased the new companion recipe book. Great resources for Stoppong Kidney Disease not just treating symptoms.

author: Barbara H. Moore
title: A resource for anyone with chronic kidney disease. A must have
body: Information that you will never get from any regular doctor. If you have chronic kidney disease I recommend this book. If you follow Leehom’s recommendations you will see an improvement in your condition.

author: Lisa Hill
title: Reduce the workload on your kidneys.
body: Very very informative review of nutrients that can harm and why. Deep discussion of overall health and other conditions that effect kidney function. Plenty of cited studies provided to backup conclusions. Sensible charts for specific foods to avoid, to limit, to substitute, and to include in a kidney-friendly diet. Not a cookbook.

author: N. Wassef
title: Wonderful source of information
body: Absolutely the best book about kidney disease on the market. Based on large number of studies and personal experiences. I would recommend that everyone with kidney disease will read it.

author: Positive Thinker
title: You Probably Need This Book
body: Kidney disease is on the rise, and is a silent killer. Imagine my surprise to learn through blood and urine tests that I, who thought I was in great health for my age, already have advanced kidney disease. I'm only into 100 pages but so far, I cannot help but be tremendously impressed. I'm not a medical doctor. For the past 3 years, my primary care doctor has sent me to have blood and urine drawn, and the report came back with a low "eGFR". The doctor only asked me if I was experiencing any problems with my kidneys -- a bit sluggish, and to my knowledge at that point, nothing unexpected given my age and certainly no pain. As it turns out this year my eGFR is what it was last year, only 57, when that of a healthy, young person is 100-120. So looking that up on reputable websites, come to find out that means Stage 3 kidney disease, and not only that, but that kidney disease is progressive and there are only 5 stages. My doctor still only asks if I have kidney pain or "problems" and I say no and that's it. The author of this book, although, like myself, not being a medical doctor either, does, like myself, believe in taking control of one's own health, and for laypeople, that means intensive self-study and research. He has done an impressive amount of that. I have books written by physicians who don't discuss a fraction of the confirmed information out there, just directing you to nephrologists. Well thanks but I prefer prevention and intervention to big pharma and surgery. Having had gall-bladder surgery years ago, in my experience, doctors are in a state of denial about the changes to your body when you lose an organ causing lasting problems that need to be managed. They don't say you could have repeat gallstones, that you could have gastrointestinal irritation, that many people, myself included, have to take anti-diarrheals and considerable fiber capsules and drinks to avoid "accidents", and this does not go away. So I would draw the line at having any kind of surgery on my kidneys. And I thank this author for this book. It is written in easy to understand language; the studies are summarized, however - if you are not medically trained or have done medical reading, just skip reading the studies, as you may misinterpret what they are saying. One caution also, whenever we read medical studies we need to know who sponsored them (Big Pharma sponsors many studies as the book The Truth About the Drug Companies by Maria Angell, former editor New England Journal of Medicine, details), how large, the study methods, and so forth. But when we see numerous studies that agree with one another and when we see a book author smart enough to even include studies, and that author's conclusions (such as about inflammation in the body being a major factor in disease) completely concur with numerous other books with citations written by physicians and specialists, then these are good indicators of trustworthy information. In only 100 pages, I've already gotten so many questions I had about kidneys and how they function answered. It is not fair to submit a review prior to reading the entire book so there will be an update to this review. Meantime, if only for the first 100 pages, it already has been worth the price. PROMISED UPDATE: I've now completed my first reading of this book; as is usual I will go back over it and make more notes, mark some passages. The book might benefit by editing for brevity and clarity, but that would depend on the reader. Some readers will enjoy Hull's conversational and down-to-earth style; others will want less digression. If I could put this entire book on a word processor, I think I'd eliminate a lot of overlap and repetition, get it down to more manageable size. I might also just summarize and cite the relevant studies, such as is done in the books of Sherry Rogers. That having been said, among all of the verbiage, I found quite important and interesting facts that were new to me even if it did take quite a bit of time to go through 500 pages. The use of statin drugs to lower cholesterol is endorsed in this book. Before deciding on whether to use these drugs, I recommend reading the books The Cholesterol Hoax by Sherry Rogers, MD, The Cholesterol Myth by Steven Sinatra, MD, and some of the other books on statin drugs available on Amazon. There are many reasons to avoid statins ... you just want to get all of the information before deciding, and this book does not explain the pros and cons of this class of drugs. This book lets us know that kidney disease is not curable and is progressive, but we can lower the demands we make on our kidneys and thus help them function better, putting us into remission. A strict low-protein diet is recommended along with a proprietary supplement developed by the author. I checked the current price on the website and it is about $180.00 per month, putting it out of my price range. The diet consists of whole, plant-based foods, essentially a vegan diet (a separate diet book is sold). In addition, regular exercise, stress reduction, and being smart about supplements and vitamins are included ... the chapter on the latter is especially interesting. To summarize Pros: If your blood tests low for eGFR, then you should read this book. A lot of helpful information for people with low kidney function. Conversational style and (except for the studies) can be understood by the average person. Cons: The most important of the studies could be summarized and cited; the rest just cited. Could be edited for brevity and clarity and repetition eliminated.

author: medusa
title: Detail oriented mayerial
body: This book would be great if I was a medical student...too technical...it was very detailed and I think I wanted language a lay person can understand without being given too much information...however, there are those who want details.🤓

author: Crazygrandma
title: This made me an INFORMED CKD patient
body: This book has been a life saver for me. Finding complete information like this isn't easy, I know, I tried. Even my nephrologist seemed impressed when he paged through it, liked all the science that was presented, and plans to get a copy for himself to check it out thoroughly. This book gives you information about the medical trials and research going on so that you can make informed decisions about your health. Should this be the only book you read on CKD? NO! READ EVERYTHING YOU CAN GET YOUR HANDS ON! But this book is what helped me when first diagnosed, was in a panic, and didn't know enough even to be able to ask questions. Doctors are much too blase' about CKD, take a "watch and see" attitude while our bodies are becoming permanently damaged. NOW is the time to act. Become informed, be your own health advocate, don't trust your life in the hands of others. It's YOUR life. Oh, and I also recommend the cook book that has recently came out.

author: Linda say burn
title: Look for the needle...
body: in the haystack. That, is...you know when you read something and keep saying to yourself "yeah, yeah, get to the point!"? That's this book. ...in this haystack. There is a mountain of research and data. Don't need it! Just TELL ME WHAT TO DO! It does get there eventually, but you have to wade through mountains of uber-dull peer-reviewed, cross-referenced, epidemiological studies to get there. Most chapters could be reduced to one page. No doubt the author is a good guy and well-intentioned, but this stuff is turgid dude!

author: WMK
title: Great info for Kidney patients!
body: It breaks down info for the average person to understand. The author was very helpful when I had a problem.

author: R. Kelley
title: Quite Comprehensive
body: Lee Hull has certainly researched the topic. I'm still reading the ebook and will for awhile since I research a lot of what he says in addition to reading other books too. I just found out today that my brother has stage 3 CKD just like me and he's been in stage 3 for several years now. Believe me, my brother has a horrible diet and I don't. Today he told me he pretty much eats frozen foods like Stouffer's meatloaf just about everyday. Are you kidding me - with stage 3 CKD? But for some reason it remains at bay in stage 3. Go figure! Bottom line, I give this book four stars only because it's an ebook and it does not allow me to enlarge the graphs and tables like other ebooks do. I have to get a magnifying glass in order to read those sections. I'd recommend the physical book not the ebook. Update: I found out how to enlarge graphs and tables. For some reason it's different in the ebooks than others I've read. Therefore, I'm up-grading to five stars. Lee Hull appears to leave no stone unturned.

author: L. Ariella Safer
title: Putting kidney disease into remission
body: I loved the book Stopping Kidney disease!! I went on the Vegan diet first and after 2 weeks my GFR increased by 3 points. After 3 months my GFR increased by 18 points, but I had decreased my protein too much and it wasn't sustainable due to fatigue. I then increased my protein to 40 grams/day. My GFR decreased by 8 points, but it was still 10 points higher than when I started. I then added in Albutrix, which allowed me to consume fewer grams of protein in my diet and fill in the rest with low nitrogen Albutrix. The one caveat with a plant based diet is it is high in potassium, so I had to start tracking it in my food diary. Then it fell back within normal limits. Now I have everything under control!!

author: Marie Chandler
title: Great Book!
body: This is a fantastic book written by a knowledgeable individual with CKD.

author: DAVID S.
title: Very well researched, but short on practical application and lack of information about author
body: I was recently diagnosed with stage 3 CKD with GFR 32. In my panicked state, I have turned to all the research I can find about kidney disease and what I might be able to do to keep it from progressing. Lee's book is a wonderful reference about what is happening in our bodies when we have CKD - very well researched and documented. And, as the other reviews have said, is VERY technical and somewhat difficult to understand. I skimmed over most of the cited references and went to the summaries. Where I find much of the information in the book very helpful, my SKEPTICISM about the book is that Lee is selling a product, Albutrix, (which is a nitrogen free albumin supplement) in his prescription for healing your kidneys. The other thing that makes me take the book with a grain of salt is there is virtually no information about Lee's background, other than that he is a CKD patient and was frustrated with the poor kidney care offered in the U.S. I have no idea what is educational background. Is he a scientist, doctor, nutritionist???? What are his credentials that make him such an authority about this subject. It did not state it in the book nor could I find any information about him on the internet. The third reason I give this book a 3 is that even though Lee gives excellent scientific information, there is very little practical application. I couldn't find lists of foods and what are good/ bad foods to eat that fit his diet. I did not learn what foods work in his prescription. So I do recommend this book for the information, but read it with some skepticism and do further research. I would also be careful about buying his product without talking to your doctor about it first.

author: Amazon Customer
title: Extremely informative
body: My go to book for kidney issues, in depth look at many issues previously unknown to me.

author: J. Sawyer
title: Wonderful!
body: THIS BOOK IS SO UNIQUELY THOROUGH! WILL SAVE LIVES!

author: S. E. Martin
title: If you have kidney disease you need to read this.
body: Even if you prefer to ignore your problem, you need to make an informed choice. You can make a huge difference in whether your disease progresses or not. The time to decide is now. After spending many years as a health professional, I know that many people prefer to die rather than change their diets. If you are not one of these people, this book is a God send. If anything can stop the progress of your disease & keep you from having to go on dialyses, it is the information gathered in this book. At the very least, following the advice given in it will slow the progression. I have seen it improve GFRs considerably.

author: Mottie
title: language/information not for initiated
body: I thought this guy who has written so many emails that "made sense" would write the same in a book which is why I bought this. Not so. too technical, not halpful at all plus I lost $4 on refund. Enough said.

author: Tina the S.
title: Stopping Kidney Disease
body: Written by a patient who has been frustrated by doctor's lack of good dietary and patient-friendly medical advice as to treat kidney disease, the author attempts to combine science (which is very hard to follow and not helpful to the lay reader, or to other patients seeking real answers) along with very confusing dietary advice...one has to sift through hundreds of pages of confusing information to find out what foods to avoid and what foods do not hurt kidney function. The book tries to do too much and thus does not provide CLEAR, easy to follow dietary information and can be a frustrating read. There is some good information there, but nearly enough.

author: Kindle Customer
title: Well written. Every chapter was a Revelation about a subject that is literally a matter of Life & Death for me and millions of other people
body: I feel more optimistic about my chances of being able to actually do something meaningful about managing my own medical issues than I ever had before. Considering the amount of information, I was able to finish reading in record time. I found myself looking forward to every new chapter and the things I would learn about something I had been dealing with for years. I highly recommend this IMPORTANT Effort to anyone who has had to deal with kidney issues and has been frustrated by it's progress

author: b. holiday
title: This book gave me hope!
body: I read this book on Kindle unlimited and thought it was so good that I bought a copy. I have made several changes to my diet and am looking forward to doing a blood test in about three months to see my improvements.

author: Amazon Customer
title: interesting

author: Customer

body: This is one of the best researched, designed, and written books I've ever read on any subject. I can't recommend it highly enough if you have or think you have kidney disease. Note to all publishers: Study the organization and presentation of information in this book. This was a pleasure to read.

author: Dixie
title: Great book

author: Bernard

If he is right and if I wait for his diet and lifestyle changes, I should be dead by the time I finish this wordy technical ad.

author: Bootsie

author: D. Watson
title: What to eat and what not to eat?!
body: I just got this book and it is not going to be a quick read. It looks like an amazing compliation of information. But knowing that diet is KEY...it's disappointing that the dietary information is not layed out simply and clearly. That is what I want to begin immediately and there doesn't appear to be anywhere to find that information is a single place. It appears it will have to be extracted as you read through this sizeable book. When every day counts it just surprises me!! But it is very clear that this is an important work for those fighting kidney disease and shocking to read how the US is rated 66th in pre-dialysis care. Shocking and shameful, so Lee's book is going to help so many people. But Lee PLEASE lay out the eating plan clearly!!

author: The Rebecca Review
title: Preserve Kidney Function Now! A Guide on How to Stop the Downward Spiral of Chronic Kidney Disease
body: If you have been referred to a nephrologist you are probably a little worried about your health. After my first appointment with my new doctor I realized that I was not going to get a lot of detailed information about managing Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD). Basically I was told to avoid Advil, to avoid salt (to manage my high blood pressure) and to drink enough water. All things I already knew. So where to turn for more information? A kindle book of course and one written by someone who has discovered how to put his kidney disease in remission. What I noticed right at the start of the book is how logical the author is and how well he's done his research. There are extensive footnotes on some chapters - 113 on one! Lee Hull also seems to be looking out for the reader by giving you all the facts you should be considering. This book answers the following questions: What type of protein is damaging to my kidneys? What will help cramps, edema and nausea? Which foods, supplements and medicines can help kidney disease? Why do I need to manage sodium, potassium and phosphorus? Why is albumin so important to survival? Should I avoid supplemental calcium and is calcium in food OK? By reading this book I also learned about acidic and alkaline foods. My favorite cheeses are very acidic. Parmesan is at 34.2 and cheddar at 26.4. So somehow I have to eat less of those if I don't want to make my kidneys work harder to balance my pH. To me this book presents the challenge of quantity of life and quality of life. If you want to live longer, then the information in this book will be helpful. If you want to live with less symptoms of disease, then this book gives a lot to think about. Mostly this book presents research studies to back up the medical information. I will admit that I fast forwarded through a lot of the research studies because they were beyond my understanding. Fortunately Lee Hull understands them and presents summaries at the end of them that were easy to understand. Basically this book recommends a vegetarian diet for the most part. I think with many illnesses increasing fruits and vegetables is the main recommendation because to fight disease we need more antioxidants. So I will do that. It was interesting to read that anxiety is part of kidney disease and I'm being treated for that as well. After reading this book I will pay way more attention to my blood pressure readings now that I know what the dangers are. I look forward to reading the next book with diet tips and recipes. Learning what to eat seems the most essential thing with this disease. My GFR is at 52 currently and so I'm in stage 3. This book indicates that I should do all I can to keep kidney disease from progressing further. ~The Rebecca Review P.S. - 12/11/2023 - I started taking acacia fiber and that made my GFR go back up to 68. I am not a doctor, nor is this medical advice. I should technically be dead by now because my GFR was in the 40s. I read a book about supplements for kidney disease and so here I am still alive! I'm going to my nurse again in a few weeks and will find out if I'm going to still live a bit longer. I am going to read another of this author's books since I want to live a bit longer. P.S. 2 - I have been using Lee Hull's amazing food guide cookbook and the pumpkin chili is amazing. I use vegetable base and water in place of the vegetable stock and it works!

author: Amazon Customer
title: Wealth of information
body: I'm 53 years old. To date, I have yet to feel the need to rate anything. This book has me inspired. I've done a ton of my own research and have never seen anything related to kidney disease put together in such a brilliant fashion. I will use this book as a blueprint for the continued management of my own kidney disease but also encourage others, otherwise healthy people to grab a copy of this book. The topics discussed apply to everyone regardless of status of health. I can only imagine the effort it took for you to complete this book. Amazing Lee Hull. Thank you.

author: Garden Gal
title: Bar None: The Most Important Kidney Disease Help Book on the Market
body: This is the most important book on kidney disease I've read. Since my husband was diagnosed with Stage III Kidney Disease, I've read quite a lot, including 6 renal recipe books. Most of them are seriously outdated and would have you believe that eating meat, fish and dairy will help stop chronic CKD. Trust me, that meal plan didn't work for my husband. As Mr. Hull points out, animal-sourced items, as well as many non-vegetable and non-fruit items, are acid-forming, but the kidneys prefer a diet low on the PRAL (Potential Renal Acid Load) list. So, for the past 10 days we've become strict vegans and consumed food higher in alkaline, lower in acid, and we're drinking alkaline water. We also upped our intake of magnesium. When his test results came back, my husband was shocked. His creatinine level had gone down, while his GFR (glomular filtration rate) had gone up 2 points since his labs from only a month ago. Hurrah! Thank you, Lee Hull! I will add to this review whether future results are negative or positive. Update: Here are my husband's latest lab results: GFR (glomular filtration rate) shot up from 32 in Jan. and 34 in March to 41.5 in May. And his creatinine decreased from 2.0 in Jan. and 1.9 in March down to 1.6 in May. We're over the moon with delight! He's still Stage III, but no longer at the bottom verging on the need for dialysis. One caveat to mention. His red blood cell count was down. I assume this was due to the lack of heme iron from animal foods in the diet. Non-heme iron from plants is less easily absorbed. We'll correct this deficit with more beans and iron-fortified cereal like original Malt-o-meal.

author: Jadecat
title: Hoping to have strong kidneys til I die
body: I had high blood pressure for 10 years. My moms oncologist mentioned I should see a nephrologist. I did. CT scan followed and my renal arteries were 90% and 40% blocked. I was shocked. I was 54, non smoker, not overweight, active. Anyways, had one renal stent put in, and fortunately I had 10 years of bloodwork I could look back on, so I checked out my GFR readings, they ranged from 72-96, so never too bad. The cardiologist said I was too young to have atherosclerotic arteries and that was that. I started learning about kidney disease as I don't know how my kidneys weren't damaged from the blockage and continuous HBP. Came across this book and ordered it. It is very informative, and while my kidney issues aren't bad at this time, I don't want them to get worse and this book is giving me a great base of information. I also have the upper range of high cholesterol (200) and glucose is nearing 100. I eat well and can't figure out why my fast food, soda drinking friends are "healthier" than me. Maybe genetics, but this book inspires me to stay ahead of any kidney disease that might be heading my way.

author: S.M.
title: This is an outstanding book and a must-read for anyone wth any stage of kidney disease!
body: Before this book was published I received a chapter at a time via email from the author with the opportunity to benefit by the information and offer feedback to the author. Then when it was finally published, I ordered a rush copy from Amazon for my Mom who was recently diagnosed with Stage 3 kidney disease. Mr. Hull has himself reversed his own kidney disease with much research and experimentation. Of all of the books that I have bought for my Mom on the subject, Mr. Hull's book is to her the bible on the subject. Don't miss out reading this book if you or a loved one is dealing with kidney disease. Mr. Hull provides a great deal of helpful information.

author: DcChick
title: Well Documented Kidney Resource
body: The best resource for kidney disease, well researched and documented, written by a man who has been successfully dealing with his own severe kidney issues for years. It has also worked successfully for me in dealing with my much less severe situation.

author: Amazon Customer
title: This book changed my life.
body: The summer of 2017 I had my yearly blood work. My doctor said my GFR was a little low and to drink more water. I googled my GFR number and found out I'm stage 3 in kidney disease. I was shocked. A few months later I came across Lee Hull, stopping kidney disease. He sent me pieces of information about his book from his website before it was published. I stopped eating so much meat. I balanced it with more vegetables and fruits. I gave up sodas. Balanced my diet following Lee Hull's book and my blood work paper. Last summer 2018 my doctor was surprised my GFR was rising. It was 33 in 2017. 2018 it was 42! There were other improvements as well. If your serious about adding years to your life this book is for you. Thank you Mr. Lee Hull for this fighting chance!

author: Donna R Graham
title: Great book and good research!
body: I have been waiting for a book like this. Our medical doctors could benefite reading it to extend their medical knowledge. I have told my doctor about it.
